title: Python Foundations Sprint (Syntax + Data Types)
problemStatement:
Welcome to Day 1 — Python Mastery Sprint.

**Context:** Before becoming a strong Software Engineer, you must be fluent in Python basics.

**What to do:**
- Learn and practice:
  - Variables
  - Data types (int, float, str, bool)
  - Type casting
  - Input/output

**Task:**
Create a Python script that:
1. Takes user name and age as input
2. Prints a formatted message
3. Converts age into integer and prints age after 5 years

**Example Output:**
Hello Aaron, you are 20 years old.
In 5 years, you will be 25.

**Submission:** Push `day1_basics.py`

title: Chunking Strategies for Long Document Processing
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Context Chunking
Real documents — research papers, manuals, articles — are too long to fit into LLM context windows. Chunking splits large documents into smaller overlapping pieces without losing meaning at the boundaries. Build a chunking experiment comparing multiple strategies on the same document to understand how chunk design affects retrieval quality.

**Tools:** Python, LangChain

**What to do:**
- Load a long text document of at least five pages — use a Wikipedia article or research paper — as a plain string variable in Python
- Implement fixed-size chunking manually in Python without LangChain: split the text every N characters using a loop so you understand the mechanics from scratch
- Implement recursive character text splitting using LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with configurable chunk_size and chunk_overlap parameters
- Experiment with chunk sizes of 100, 300, 500, and 1000 characters with overlaps of 0, 50, and 100 characters and run the same retrieval query against each configuration
- Compare the retrieved chunks across all configurations for the same query and print which configuration returns the most contextually complete result
- Find three specific examples where a sentence is split mid-thought across chunk boundaries and document the exact character position where each split happened
- Write a recommendation documenting which chunk size and overlap setting produced the best retrieval quality for your document and the reasoning behind that choice

**Submission:**
- Chunking experiment notebook comparing retrieval quality across twelve configurations, three documented boundary failure examples, and a written chunking strategy recommendation

linkedinTemplate:
Chunking is one of those things that sounds trivial until you try to do it well.

Split too aggressively — 200 token chunks — and you lose context. A sentence that depends on the paragraph before it becomes meaningless in isolation. Split too conservatively — 2000 token chunks — and you drown the LLM in irrelevant context and blow your latency budget.

Today I implemented and compared four strategies: fixed-size chunking, sentence-boundary chunking, paragraph-aware chunking, and sliding window with overlap. I ran the same 10 queries through retrieval using each strategy and manually compared the quality of what came back.

Sliding window with overlap consistently outperformed fixed-size on multi-sentence questions. The overlap is not waste — it is continuity.

title: Understanding Embeddings as Semantic Coordinates
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Dense Embeddings
TF-IDF vectors fail when words are different but meanings are the same. Embeddings solve this by encoding semantic meaning as dense numerical coordinates in high-dimensional space. Build workflows that generate, compare, and cluster embeddings to deeply understand how they represent meaning beyond keyword overlap.

- Use K-means clustering on the 20 embedding vectors to group sentences into four clusters and verify that cluster assignments align with the original four topics

title: Build a Semantic Search Engine

Metadata filtering turned a generic document search into something that actually understands context.

Without metadata, my retrieval system treats every chunk equally. A question about "2023 revenue" retrieves financial chunks from 2019 through 2024. With metadata filtering, you can constrain retrieval to the right year, author, document type, or department before similarity scoring even runs.

Built a three-field metadata schema, tagged every document in my corpus, and rewrote the retrieval logic to accept optional filter parameters. title: Why LLMs Hallucinate and How to Measure It
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Hallucination Understanding and Detection
Hallucination is not a random bug — it is a predictable consequence of how language models generate text by predicting the next most likely token rather than retrieving verified facts. Understanding the mechanics of hallucination is essential before you can systematically reduce it. Build a hallucination measurement experiment that quantifies how often and why your LLM fabricates information.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API

**What to do:**
- Create a dataset of 20 factual questions where you know the ground-truth correct answers — cover at least three different knowledge domains to test breadth
- Run each question through GPT-4o-mini with no context and no retrieval, recording every response verbatim in a structured results dictionary
- Manually evaluate each response and classify it as correct, partially correct, or hallucinated using explicit criteria you define before scoring begins
- Categorise hallucinated responses by type: fabricated specific facts, outdated information presented as current, confident wrong answer, or plausible-sounding but unverifiable claim
- Run the same 20 questions through your Day 15 RAG pipeline and record all responses in the same structured format
- Calculate a hallucination rate percentage for both conditions — no context and with RAG — and compare the two numbers
- Build a multi-signal hallucination detector in Python with four independent checks: (1) unsupported claim detection — flag any specific numerical claim, named entity, or date in the response that does not appear in the retrieved context; (2) retrieval overlap check — compute token-level overlap between the response and the retrieved chunks using Jaccard similarity and flag responses below 0.15 overlap; (3) contradiction detection — send a secondary LLM call asking 'Does this response contradict the provided context? Answer yes or no.' and flag positives; (4) citation absence check — flag any response that makes verifiable factual assertions but contains no reference to a source document or passage. Combine all four signals into a final confidence score and print a per-response breakdown

**Submission:**
- Hallucination measurement report with 20-question test dataset, categorised failure table, hallucination rate comparison between no-context and RAG conditions, and four-signal rule-based detection script with per-response breakdown output

LLMs hallucinate in patterns. Once you know the patterns, you can actually measure them.

Today I built a four-signal hallucination detector — not based on vague hedging phrases, but on concrete structural signals. Signal one: unsupported claim detection, flagging any specific number, date, or named entity in the response that does not appear anywhere in the retrieved context. Signal two: retrieval overlap, measuring Jaccard similarity between the response and the context and flagging responses below 0.15. Signal three: contradiction detection, using a secondary LLM call to ask whether the response contradicts the context. Compared hallucination rates on 20 questions: no-context condition vs RAG. The gap was larger than I expected.

title: Designing Production-Ready AI APIs
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** API Design for AI Systems
AI APIs face unique design challenges that standard REST APIs do not: streaming responses, token limits, retry logic for flaky LLM calls, and expensive operations that must be short-circuited before invalid inputs reach the model. Design and build a production-grade AI API with proper validation, error handling, streaming, and structured error codes.

**Tools:** Python, FastAPI, Pydantic

**What to do:**
- Redesign your Day 20 FastAPI endpoint using Pydantic request and response models with typed fields, field validators, and example values in the schema
- Add input validation that rejects queries under five characters, over 1000 characters, or containing only whitespace or special characters — each returning a descriptive error message with an error code
- Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for OpenAI API rate limit errors using a try-except loop with configurable sleep intervals and a maximum retry count
- Add streaming response support using FastAPI's StreamingResponse so the first tokens appear in the client before the full generation is complete
- Implement a 15-second request timeout: if the full pipeline does not complete in time, return a structured partial response with a LLM_TIMEOUT error code rather than hanging indefinitely
- Define three structured error codes with consistent JSON shapes: RETRIEVAL_FAILURE, LLM_TIMEOUT, and INPUT_INVALID — each including code, message, and request_id fields

title: Building Conversation Memory Systems
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Stateful AI Conversations
LLMs are stateless — each API call forgets the previous one entirely. Real assistant products require conversation memory so follow-up questions like 'what did you mean by that?' work correctly without the user repeating context. Build a memory system that maintains conversation state across multiple turns without overflowing the token limit.

**Tools:** Python, LangChain, OpenAI API, FastAPI

**What to do:**
- Implement a ConversationHistory class that stores message history per session as a list of role-content pairs and exposes append(), get_context(), and clear() methods
- Modify your FastAPI endpoint to accept an optional session_id parameter and maintain a separate ConversationHistory instance per session in a server-side dictionary
- Build a context injection strategy that includes only the last five turns of conversation history in each prompt to avoid exceeding the model's token limit
- Test multi-turn conversations by sending a sequence of five messages where turns three and five use pronouns that only make sense with memory of the earlier turns
- Implement memory window truncation: when a session exceeds 10 turns, summarise the oldest five turns into a single context block using an LLM call and replace them with that summary
- Run the same five-turn conversation with and without memory active and compare the quality of the follow-up answers
- Quantify the memory trade-off: calculate the additional token cost per turn at your current window size and estimate the monthly cost difference at 1000 daily users averaging 10 turns per session

**Submission:**
- Session-aware AI assistant with ConversationHistory class, memory window truncation, five-turn conversation test outputs with and without memory, and a written token cost analysis

Built conversation memory today. The assistant can now follow a thread across multiple turns.

Without memory, every message is isolated. "What about the second point?" returns confusion — there is no second point in context. With a properly managed conversation window, follow-up questions, pronoun references, and clarifications all work correctly.

Implemented sliding window memory — keep the last N turns — and context compression for long sessions. The compressed summary approach was more interesting than I expected: the LLM summarises its own prior conversation to fit within the context budget.

The failure mode worth noting: hallucinated conversation. If you inject context incorrectly, the model invents prior exchanges that never happened. Took two debugging sessions to figure out why it was doing that.

After two weeks of building, your AI system is functional but probably structurally messy. Hardcoded values, duplicated logic, and missing tests accumulate quietly and slow down every future day. title: Introduction to AI Agents and the ReAct Pattern
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** AI Agents
A standard LLM answers a question in one step. An AI agent plans, uses tools, observes results, and decides what to do next autonomously. Understand agent architecture from the inside by building a ReAct-pattern agent manually — without a framework — so you can see exactly how reasoning and acting combine and precisely where autonomous decision-making breaks down.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API

**What to do:**
- Study the ReAct pattern and implement the full loop manually in Python: the LLM produces a Thought, names an Action with parameters, you execute the action, feed the Observation back into the next prompt, and repeat until the LLM outputs a Final Answer
- Implement three tool functions: a calculator(expression) that evaluates arithmetic, a search_docs(query) that retrieves from your Day 11 knowledge base, and a get_today() that returns the current date as a string
- Write a tool_registry dictionary mapping tool names to functions and build a dispatch function that parses the LLM's named action and routes it to the correct tool
- Run the agent on five multi-step problems that require using more than one tool in sequence — for example, a question that needs a document lookup followed by a calculation on the retrieved value
- Print the full Thought → Action → Observation trace for every step of every problem so the entire reasoning chain is visible and inspectable
- Identify and document where the agent goes wrong: looping without progress, hallucinating a tool output it never actually received, or selecting the wrong tool for the task
- Write a short analysis of what makes an agent reliable versus unpredictable, grounded specifically in the failure patterns you observed during your five test problems

**Submission:**
- Manual ReAct agent with three tools and a dispatch function, full reasoning traces for all five test problems, documented failure patterns, and written reliability analysis

Agents are a completely different way of thinking about AI systems. I did not fully appreciate that until today.

An agent does not just answer a question. It reasons about what to do, picks a tool, runs it, looks at the result, decides whether to continue or stop. The ReAct pattern — Reason, Act, Observe, repeat — makes that loop explicit.

Built a basic agent from scratch using just Python and the OpenAI API: no framework magic, just the loop itself. The agent can call two tools: a web search stub and a calculator. Watched it chain three tool calls to answer a question that a single LLM call would have hallucinated.

The hard part is not the mechanics. It is writing tools that reliably return structured output the model can act on.

title: Structured Tool Calling with the OpenAI Functions API
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Structured Tool Calling
Day 26's manual ReAct agent parsed tool calls from raw LLM text output — fragile and error-prone when the model formats its output slightly differently. OpenAI's function calling API makes tool use reliable by having the model output structured JSON specifying exactly which function to call and with what arguments. Rebuild your agent using function calling and measure reliability directly against the Day 26 approach.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API, FastAPI

**What to do:**
- Define your four tools as JSON schemas following OpenAI's function schema format: search_documents, get_weather_stub, calculate, and get_today — each with name, description, and a parameters object with typed fields
- Build a tool execution loop: send the user query to OpenAI with the function schemas, detect when the response contains a tool_calls field, execute the matching Python function with the provided arguments, append the result as a tool message, and continue until a final text response arrives
- Run the same five multi-step problems from Day 26 using function calling and compare whether the model selects the same tools and produces the same final answers
- Test a problem requiring two sequential tool calls — for example search for a document then calculate something from what it found — and print the full exchange showing both tool calls
- Find three cases where the model passes incorrect or missing arguments to a tool and write a validation wrapper that catches these before they reach the function
- Expose the agent as a FastAPI POST /agent endpoint that accepts a question and returns the final answer plus the complete list of tool calls made during the interaction
- Write a comparison document explaining the specific reliability differences between the Day 26 manual text-parsing approach and the structured function calling approach with concrete examples

**Submission:**
- Function-calling agent with four tool schemas, FastAPI /agent endpoint, five-problem comparison against Day 26, argument validation wrapper, and reliability comparison document

title: Multi-Step AI Workflows with State Management
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Stateful Multi-Step Agent Workflows
Real-world AI tasks rarely finish in a single step. Generating a research report requires searching, extracting key points, synthesising findings, and formatting output — four sequential steps where each depends on the output of the last. Build a multi-step workflow with persistent state so a partially completed run can resume from the last checkpoint rather than restarting from scratch.

**Tools:** Python, LangChain, OpenAI API

**What to do:**
- Design a four-step research workflow and implement each step as a standalone Python function: search_sources(topic), extract_key_points(chunks), synthesise_findings(points), and format_report(synthesis)
- Build a WorkflowState dataclass that carries the topic, retrieved chunks, extracted points, synthesis text, and final report as fields — passed between every step
- Wire the four step functions together in a run_workflow(topic) orchestrator that passes the updated state through each step sequentially
- Implement checkpoint saving: after each step completes, serialise the WorkflowState to a JSON file on disk named after the step so a crashed run can restart from the last saved point
- Test resume functionality by intentionally raising an exception during step three, then modify the orchestrator to detect the checkpoint file and skip already-completed steps on restart
- Add step-level error handling: if any step raises an exception, log the error with the step name and the current state snapshot, then exit cleanly with a partial results file
- Run the complete workflow on three different research topics and compare the structure and quality of the three generated reports

**Submission:**
- Multi-step research workflow with WorkflowState dataclass, checkpoint saving and tested resume, step-level error handling, and three generated research reports from different topics

Multi-step AI workflows with state that actually persists across steps — that is a different class of system.

Today I built a four-step research workflow: plan queries, search, synthesise, write report. Each step hands structured state to the next. If the synthesis step decides the retrieved information is insufficient, it loops back. That conditional routing is what makes it an agent workflow rather than just a chain.

The state management was the hardest part. Getting each step to read cleanly from the shared state dictionary without stomping on each other's output required more careful schema design than I expected.

title: Building AI Evaluation Systems
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** AI Evaluation and Measurement
You cannot improve what you cannot measure. Automated evaluation systems that objectively score your AI system's outputs are a core production engineering skill — they are what separate teams that iterate based on data from teams that iterate based on gut feel. Build an LLM-as-judge evaluation framework that scores your assistant's answers and runs automatically as a regression test before any deployment.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API

**What to do:**
- Define three evaluation dimensions with precise criteria: groundedness meaning the answer is supported only by retrieved context with no fabricated additions, correctness meaning the answer matches known ground truth, and completeness meaning the full question is answered with nothing important omitted
- Build an llm_judge(question, context, answer, ground_truth) function that sends a structured scoring prompt to GPT-4o-mini and returns scores from 1 to 5 for each dimension as a parsed JSON object
- Create a labelled evaluation dataset of 20 question-answer pairs for your Day 20 knowledge assistant where you have written the correct ground-truth answer for each question manually
- Run your assistant on all 20 questions and pass each response through llm_judge(), collecting all scores in a results dictionary
- Calculate aggregate scores per dimension — average groundedness, average correctness, average completeness — and identify which dimension is lowest and by how much
- Build a regression_test_runner() function that runs the full 20-question suite, compares each dimension score against a stored baseline, and prints PASS or FAIL with the specific dimension and score drop if any threshold is breached
- Document the limitations of LLM-as-judge evaluation honestly: where the judge can be wrong, which answer types it cannot score reliably, and when human evaluation is still necessary

**Submission:**
- Automated evaluation framework with llm_judge() function, 20-question labelled dataset, aggregate score report per dimension, regression_test_runner(), and evaluation limitations document

You cannot improve what you do not measure. Today I built the measurement system.

Automated AI evaluation using an LLM judge sounds circular until you think about it carefully. The judge is not assessing creativity — it is applying a consistent rubric you define: correctness, groundedness, completeness. You validate the judge's scores against your own manual ratings. If they correlate well enough, you can run thousands of evaluations automatically.

Built scorers for three dimensions, validated them on 20 manually-scored examples, got 87% agreement with my own ratings. That is good enough to use for regression testing.

The most uncomfortable result: my system's average correctness score is lower than I thought it was.

title: How Production AI Systems Handle Scale
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Scalability Architecture
A system that works for one user often breaks at ten. Production AI systems face scaling challenges specific to their stack: LLM calls are slow and expensive, embedding generation is compute-heavy, and many users ask similar questions that waste budget on repeated API calls. Add caching and asynchronous processing to your assistant so it handles concurrent load without proportionally increasing cost.

**Tools:** Python, FastAPI, Redis

**What to do:**
- Install Redis locally and add embedding vector caching: if a query string has been embedded in the last hour, return the cached vector without calling the OpenAI Embeddings API
- Add semantic response caching: compute cosine similarity between a new query's embedding and all cached query embeddings — if similarity exceeds 0.95, return the cached response directly without calling the LLM
- Convert your FastAPI endpoint handlers to use async/await throughout so the server can handle multiple incoming requests concurrently rather than processing them one at a time
- Move long-running research workflows into FastAPI BackgroundTasks: the endpoint returns a job_id immediately and the client polls a GET /research/status/{job_id} endpoint for completion
- Load test your endpoint by sending 10 simultaneous requests using Python's threading module and measure how many complete successfully versus time out
- Measure average response time for the same query before caching, after embedding caching, and after full response caching — print the three latency numbers side by side
- Document the cache invalidation problem in writing: when your knowledge base changes, which cached responses become stale, how you would detect this, and what your invalidation strategy would be

**Submission:**
- Scaled AI backend with Redis embedding cache, semantic response cache, async endpoints, background task job system with polling endpoint, load test results, and cache invalidation analysis

My AI backend was blocking on every request. Today I fixed that and also made it dramatically cheaper to run.

Added Redis caching at two levels: embedding cache (same query string → return cached vector, skip the API call) and semantic response cache (similar query → return cached response if cosine similarity exceeds threshold). Then rewrote the endpoint handlers with async/await throughout and moved long-running workflows into background tasks.

Load tested with 10 concurrent requests before and after. Before: 7 of 10 timed out. After: 10 of 10 completed, average latency down 65%.

The cache invalidation note I wrote down: when the knowledge base changes, every cached response that cited a stale document is now wrong. That is a real problem and I do not have a clean solution yet.

title: Latency Optimisation for AI Pipelines
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Performance Optimisation
Users abandon AI products that feel slow. Research consistently shows three seconds is the point at which users assume something is broken. Systematically profile every step of your AI pipeline, quantify exactly where time is being spent, and apply parallelisation, model selection, and streaming to reduce latency without dropping output quality below your Day 29 baseline.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API, FastAPI

**What to do:**
- Wrap every step of your pipeline with Python's time.perf_counter() and log the start and end time for each step: embedding, FAISS search, prompt construction, LLM generation, and response formatting
- Run 20 queries through the timed pipeline and calculate average time per step — print a percentage breakdown showing which step owns what fraction of total pipeline time
- Write a specific, testable optimisation hypothesis for your two slowest steps explaining what change you expect to help and by how much
- Parallelise embedding generation and any other independent I/O operations using asyncio.gather() and measure the reduction in total pipeline time
- Swap to gpt-4o-mini for any sub-steps where full model capability is not needed — run the Day 29 eval suite to confirm quality does not drop below your baseline scores
- Verify that streaming is active end-to-end: measure time-to-first-token separately from time-to-full-response for 10 queries and confirm time-to-first-token is consistently under two seconds
- Produce a before-and-after latency comparison table showing every optimisation applied, the time saving from each, and the quality score impact measured by the eval suite

**Submission:**
- Performance profiling report with per-step time percentage breakdown, parallelised async pipeline, model swap with eval score comparison, streaming verification measurements, and before-and-after optimisation table

Measured every millisecond in my pipeline today. The results were not what I expected.

Embedding generation: 23% of total latency. FAISS retrieval: 4%. LLM generation: 71%. Context window overflow is a bigger cost driver than I realised — the more tokens I send, the slower and more expensive every call gets.

Parallelised embedding and retrieval using asyncio.gather(). Swapped gpt-4o for gpt-4o-mini on the query rewriting sub-step. Both changes together cut average latency from 8.2 seconds to 4.7 seconds with no degradation on the Day 29 eval suite.

Time-to-first-token is now 1.1 seconds. That is the number users actually feel.

title: Debugging AI Systems Systematically
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** AI System Debugging
AI system bugs are different from traditional software bugs. The system runs without errors but returns wrong answers. Systematic debugging requires trace logging, component isolation, and reproducible failure scripts — not reading stack traces. Build a debugging workflow around real failures from your eval suite using LangSmith for full execution tracing.

**Tools:** Python, LangSmith, OpenAI API

**What to do:**
- Set up LangSmith tracing by adding the LangSmith callback to your LangChain calls so every pipeline execution generates a full trace viewable in the LangSmith dashboard
- Find three specific failing queries from your Day 29 evaluation — responses that scored below 2 on correctness or groundedness — and reproduce each one deliberately
- For each failure, open the LangSmith trace and identify exactly which step produced the wrong output: a bad retrieved chunk, a prompt that ignored the context, or generation that drifted from the facts
- Build a component isolation test for each failure: run the retrieval step alone, then prompt construction alone, then generation alone — printing the input and output at each stage to pinpoint where the error enters
- Add structured JSON logging to every pipeline step using Python's logging module: each log entry must include timestamp, session_id, step_name, input summary, output summary, and latency in milliseconds
- Fix two of the three reproduced failures by adjusting chunking configuration, system prompt wording, or retrieval threshold — then re-run the eval suite to confirm the fix improves those scores without regressing others
- Write a debugging runbook: a numbered step-by-step procedure you will follow every time an AI system returns a wrong answer, based on what you learned from tracing these three failures

**Submission:**
- LangSmith traces for three reproduced failures, component isolation test scripts, structured JSON logging implementation, two fixed failures with eval score before-and-after, and a debugging runbook

title: Prompt Injection and AI Safety Engineering
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** AI Security and Safety
AI systems that accept user input are vulnerable to prompt injection — carefully crafted inputs that override your system prompt and make the model behave in ways you never intended. Build a systematic attack suite targeting your own assistant, measure which attacks succeed, then implement defence layers and re-measure how many you can block.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API, FastAPI

**What to do:**
- Design 10 prompt injection attack payloads covering four categories: direct role override attempts, instruction leakage requests asking the model to reveal its system prompt, jailbreak attempts using fictional framing, and indirect injection via document chunks that contain embedded instructions
- Run each payload against your current system and classify each result as successful injection, partial compliance, or successfully blocked — print the raw model response for each case
- Implement an input sanitisation function that scans incoming queries for known injection patterns: phrases like 'ignore previous instructions', 'your system prompt is', and role-override keywords — sanitise or reject on match
- Implement output validation: after every LLM response, scan the output for patterns suggesting the model revealed its system prompt or accepted a new persona
- Add OpenAI's moderation endpoint as a pre-call safety check: flag any input that scores above the threshold in the harassment, self-harm, or hate categories and return an error instead of calling the LLM
- Re-run all 10 attack payloads after adding your three defence layers and count how many are now blocked versus before
- Write a residual risk document listing which attack vectors are still partially effective, why eliminating them completely is architecturally difficult, and what you would do next if this were a product with real security requirements

**Submission:**
- 10-payload attack suite with before-and-after success rate table, input sanitisation function, output validation check, moderation endpoint integration, and residual risk document

Built an adversarial test suite today and found out my AI assistant can be made to do things I did not intend.

Prompt injection, jailbreak attempts, system prompt extraction, role override attacks — these are not theoretical. I ran 20 attack variations against my system, documented which ones succeeded, and built defences for the highest-severity failures.

The most interesting finding: indirect prompt injection through document content. If an attacker can insert text into a document in your knowledge base, they can influence the LLM's behaviour through retrieved context rather than through the user query. That is harder to defend against than direct injection.

Implemented layered input validation, system prompt hardening, and output filtering. Re-ran the 20 attack variants after defences. Pass rate improved from 45% to 91%.

title: Production Hardening and Reliability Engineering
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Production Readiness
Production AI systems require explicit reliability standards before users depend on them. A system that crashes under load, hangs when the OpenAI API is slow, or returns silent errors is not production-ready no matter how impressive the answers are. Harden your assistant with circuit breakers, health checks, graceful degradation, and a full integration test suite.

**Tools:** Python, FastAPI, pytest

**What to do:**
- Implement a circuit breaker for your OpenAI API calls: track consecutive failures in a counter and if three consecutive calls fail, stop sending requests for 60 seconds and return a clear 'service temporarily unavailable' message
- Add a GET /health endpoint that actively checks three things: FAISS index is loaded and queryable with a test vector, Redis is reachable with a ping, and the OpenAI API responds to a minimal test call — return a structured JSON response with the status of each check
- Implement graceful degradation: if FAISS search throws an exception, fall back to your Day 11 TF-IDF keyword retrieval before returning an error — the user gets a lower-quality answer rather than a failure message
- Write a pytest integration test suite with five tests that cover the full pipeline end-to-end using mocked OpenAI API responses so tests run without hitting the real API or incurring costs
- Configure Python's logging module with DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, and ERROR levels across all modules — verify that setting log level to WARNING suppresses debug output cleanly in a simulated production environment
- Run your complete Day 29 evaluation suite against the hardened system and record the scores as your official production quality baseline
- Write a 10-item deployment checklist listing every condition that must be true before you would call this system production-ready — each item must be specific and verifiable, not generic

**Submission:**
- Hardened AI backend with circuit breaker, structured /health endpoint with three checks, graceful degradation fallback, five passing integration tests, configured logging, eval baseline scores, and 10-item deployment checklist

title: Deploy Your AI Backend to the Cloud
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Cloud Deployment
Running locally is not deploying. Real AI systems need to be accessible over the internet with environment isolation, secret management, and automatic restarts on failure. Containerise your FastAPI AI backend and deploy it to Railway so it has a live public URL that anyone can call — and verify it behaves identically to your local environment.

**Tools:** Python, FastAPI, Railway, Docker

**What to do:**
- Write a production Dockerfile for your FastAPI application: choose a slim Python base image, copy only necessary files, install dependencies from requirements.txt, and set the CMD to start uvicorn with the correct host and port
- Build the Docker image locally with docker build, run it with docker run mapping the correct port, then send a test request to confirm it behaves identically to your local development environment
- Move every secret and configuration value — API keys, model names, similarity thresholds — to environment variables and grep the codebase to confirm no secrets remain hardcoded
- Push the repository to GitHub and connect it to Railway — configure Railway to build from your Dockerfile and trigger automatic re-deploys on every push to the main branch
- Set all required environment variables in the Railway dashboard and verify the deployed /health endpoint returns HTTP 200 from the public URL
- Run your full 15-query test suite against the deployed public URL instead of localhost and confirm all responses match the local baseline
- Trigger a re-deploy by pushing a trivial commit and verify that new requests continue to be served without downtime during the rolling restart

**Submission:**
- Dockerised AI backend deployed on Railway with a live public URL, environment variable configuration, 15-query test results against the deployed URL, and confirmed zero-downtime re-deploy

title: Deploy Your AI Frontend
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Frontend Deployment and API Integration
A deployed backend without a deployed frontend is still not a product that real users can access. Deploy your Next.js frontend to Vercel, connect it to your live Railway backend URL, configure CORS so browser requests are not blocked, and verify the complete user journey works end-to-end from a real browser over the public internet.

**Tools:** Next.js, Vercel, React

**What to do:**
- Replace every hardcoded localhost URL in your Next.js project with the NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL environment variable so the frontend can point to different backends across environments
- Add CORS middleware to your FastAPI backend that explicitly allows requests from your Vercel deployment domain
- Connect your GitHub repository to Vercel and deploy the Next.js frontend with automatic re-deployments triggered on every push to main
- Set the NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL environment variable in the Vercel dashboard pointing to your Railway backend's public URL
- Test the full user journey from the Vercel-deployed frontend: submit a query, watch the streaming response arrive, and verify source citations appear correctly
- Open the deployed frontend on a mobile browser and document any layout or usability issues you find — fix at least one before the day ends
- Measure end-to-end latency from the browser by recording time from clicking submit to seeing the first streaming token — compare it to your Day 32 local latency baseline

**Submission:**

title: Collecting and Structuring Real User Feedback
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** User Feedback Engineering
Automated evaluation tells you how your system performs on your own test cases. Real user feedback tells you how it performs on actual user needs — which are always different and often surprising. Build a feedback collection system that captures user ratings, stores them persistently, and exposes an analytics endpoint so you can track quality trends over real usage.

**Tools:** Python, FastAPI, SQLite

**What to do:**
- Add thumbs-up and thumbs-down feedback buttons to your Next.js frontend below every AI response — each button must visually confirm to the user that their rating was received
- Build a POST /feedback endpoint in FastAPI that accepts and stores: session_id, the full query text, a 100-character excerpt of the response, the rating as 1 or -1, and a timestamp
- Create a SQLite database with a feedback table and verify ratings submitted through the deployed frontend appear correctly in the database
- Build a GET /feedback/summary endpoint that queries the database and returns: total interaction count, positive rating percentage, the five most common query topic words, and the three lowest-rated response excerpts
- Share your live product URL with five people who are not developers and ask them to use it naturally without any guidance or instructions from you
- After they finish, retrieve their feedback records from the database and identify a pattern: what type of query received the most thumbs-down ratings
- Write a short analysis documenting the top two user needs that your current system handles poorly, with specific evidence drawn from the feedback records

**Submission:**
- Feedback collection system with SQLite storage, POST /feedback and GET /feedback/summary endpoints, feedback data from five real non-developer users, and written analysis of the patterns found

title: Iterate on Your AI Product Based on Evidence
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Product Iteration Engineering
Feedback without action is just data collection. Convert the user feedback and failure patterns from Day 38 into concrete, measurable engineering improvements. The discipline here is prioritising based on evidence — not fixing what seems interesting, but fixing what the data shows has the biggest impact on real user experience.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API, FastAPI, LangChain

**What to do:**
- Combine all failure modes from Day 38 user feedback and your Day 29 evaluation into a single prioritised list ranked by frequency of occurrence and impact on user satisfaction
- Select the two highest-priority failures and write a specific root-cause hypothesis for each — for example 'users asking follow-up questions get wrong answers because the memory window does not preserve enough context from earlier turns'
- Implement a targeted fix for each of the two selected failures and document the exact code change: which file, which function, what changed, and why you expect the change to help
- Re-run your Day 29 evaluation suite after each fix and record the score changes — confirm the fix improved the targeted dimension without regressing any other dimension
- Update the conversation memory window to preserve seven turns instead of five and run the multi-turn conversation test from Day 24 to verify pronoun references now resolve correctly
- Add an inline 'Is this answer helpful? Yes or No' prompt appended to any response where the highest retrieval score was below 0.4, signalling to the user that the system is less confident than usual
- Deploy the updated version to Railway and Vercel, verify both deployments are live and healthy, and collect five new feedback ratings to confirm the fixes improved the experience

**Submission:**
- Prioritised failure list, two implemented and documented fixes, eval score comparison before and after each fix, updated memory window test results, and confirmed production deployment with new feedback data

Technical ability that cannot be communicated clearly is invisible to employers and collaborators. title: Define Your AI Product Problem and Target User
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Product Discovery and Problem Definition
The final 20 days are for building one real, personalised AI product. The quality of what you ship depends entirely on the quality of the problem you define. A vague problem produces a vague product. Write a specific, solvable problem statement with a real user, measurable success criteria, and a clear technical approach before writing a single line of code.

**Tools:** Notion, Excalidraw, GitHub

**What to do:**
- Choose a specific domain where you have genuine knowledge or personal interest — examples include legal research, medical education, customer support automation, code review assistance, content creation, or personal finance guidance
- Write a problem statement in this exact format: 'Users currently do X manually which takes Y amount of time and produces Z quality. An AI system could reduce this to W time with Q quality by doing V'
- Define three measurable success criteria that are specific enough to be tested — for example 'correctly answers 8 out of 10 domain-specific questions' rather than 'gives good answers'
- Write 10 realistic example user queries that your product must handle correctly — these become your Day 50 evaluation set so make them genuinely representative of user intent
- Research three existing tools that partially solve the same problem and write one sentence for each explaining specifically how your approach differs
- Identify the biggest technical risk in your product — the single component most likely to fail — and write a one-paragraph mitigation plan for it
- Write a one-page product brief consolidating everything: problem statement, target user description, solution approach, three success criteria, technical risk and mitigation, and a rough list of required components

**Submission:**
- Product brief document with formatted problem statement, 10 example user queries, competitive differentiation notes, technical risk and mitigation plan, and required component list

title: Design Your AI Product System Architecture
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** System Architecture Design
Architecture decisions made on Day 42 will constrain every day that follows. Design your AI product's complete system architecture before writing any implementation code — mapping every component, data flow, external dependency, and storage layer, and documenting the trade-offs you are consciously making rather than discovering later.

**Tools:** Excalidraw, Notion, GitHub

**What to do:**
- Draw a complete system architecture diagram showing every component as named boxes, every data flow as labelled arrows, every external API, and every storage layer
- Write a data model document: what data your product needs, where it comes from, how it is stored and indexed, and how the knowledge base is updated when new information arrives
- Choose your full tech stack and document a specific reason for each choice — not 'it is popular' but a concrete reason it fits this product's constraints such as latency requirements, data format, or cost
- Identify three architectural trade-offs you are consciously making and write one sentence explaining each: what you chose, what you gave up, and why that trade-off is acceptable for your use case
- Write the API contract for your core endpoints: HTTP method, path, request body schema, response body schema, and the error codes each endpoint can return
- Build a component dependency map listing which components must be built and working before others can be tested — this becomes your build order for Days 43 through 53
- Share your architecture diagram with one peer and incorporate at least one piece of substantive feedback into the design before finalising it

**Submission:**
- System architecture diagram, data model document, tech stack rationale, three documented trade-offs, API contract specification, component dependency map, and note documenting peer feedback incorporated

title: Build Your Knowledge Base and Data Ingestion Pipeline
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Data Engineering for AI Products
Your AI product's output quality ceiling is set by your data pipeline's quality. Before building any user-facing feature, the data foundation must be solid — reliable ingestion, preprocessing appropriate for your content type, high-quality embeddings, and a queryable FAISS index. Build the complete knowledge base pipeline for your specific product.

**Tools:** Python, LangChain, OpenAI Embeddings API, FAISS

**What to do:**
- Collect the actual data your product will operate on — a minimum of 50 documents or equivalent text volume — in the format they will arrive in from your real source
- Write an ingestion script that loads your documents and applies format-specific preprocessing for your content type: strip HTML tags, normalise whitespace, remove boilerplate headers, or handle PDF page breaks as appropriate
- Design a chunking strategy specifically suited to your document type rather than using default settings — consider whether your documents have natural section boundaries and what chunk size preserves the context your queries need
- Generate embeddings for all chunks, build a FAISS index, and print the total document count, total chunk count, average chunk size, and total ingestion time
- Run 10 of your Day 41 example queries against the index and manually review whether the top retrieved chunk is the correct one for each query — flag every query where the top result is clearly wrong
- Write an incremental_update(new_docs) function that embeds new documents and adds them to the existing FAISS index without rebuilding it from scratch
- Calculate and document three cost figures: total embedding cost for the initial ingestion, estimated monthly cost to re-embed the full knowledge base, and projected cost at 10x current document volume

**Submission:**
- Data ingestion pipeline with format-specific preprocessing, FAISS index with logged metrics, 10-query manual retrieval review with flagged failures, incremental_update() function, and cost documentation

title: Define MVP Scope and Build the Core AI Loop
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** MVP Engineering
MVP scope discipline is the difference between shipping in two weeks and never shipping. Define the minimum feature set that proves your product's core value, then build the single most important AI interaction function — before touching any secondary infrastructure, UI, or nice-to-have features.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API, FastAPI

**What to do:**
- List every feature your product could eventually have — aim for at least 10 distinct capabilities — then mark each as MVP or post-MVP based on whether the core value is provable without it
- Write one sentence defining the single most important function your product performs — the one without which the product has no value proposition at all
- Build that core function as a standalone Python function called core_ai_loop(user_input) that takes user input and returns the primary AI output your product is designed to produce
- Test core_ai_loop() against all 10 of your Day 41 example queries and manually score each result as good, acceptable, or poor with a one-sentence note for each poor result
- Wrap core_ai_loop() in a FastAPI POST /ask endpoint with basic Pydantic input validation and a structured error response schema
- Send a curl request to the running endpoint and print the response — verify it returns a correct, complete response before writing any other code
- Write a scope decision document listing every feature excluded from the MVP and one sentence explaining why each exclusion is the right call for now

**Submission:**
- core_ai_loop() function, FastAPI /ask endpoint, 10-query manual score results with notes on poor responses, curl verification output, and scope decision document with excluded features justified

title: Build the Backend Infrastructure for Your AI Product
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Backend Architecture Implementation
The Day 44 MVP proved the core AI loop works. Now build the backend infrastructure that turns it into a real system: session management, request logging, structured API surface, rate limiting, and feedback storage. This infrastructure is what makes the product debuggable, measurable, and improvable after launch.

**Tools:** Python, FastAPI, Pydantic, SQLite

**What to do:**
- Design the full API surface for your product by listing every endpoint you will need with its HTTP method, path, input schema, output schema, and purpose — use this as your build checklist
- Implement session management: assign a UUID session_id to each new user and store a separate conversation history per session in a server-side dictionary
- Add request and response logging to SQLite: every API call must log session_id, query text, response excerpt, pipeline latency in milliseconds, retrieval score, and timestamp
- Implement rate limiting using an in-memory counter per session_id: allow a maximum of 20 requests per hour and return HTTP 429 with a plain-language message when exceeded
- Build a feedback endpoint tailored to your product — define what a 'good' response means for your specific use case and what data you need to capture to measure it
- Add API key authentication using an x-api-key header check so the API is not publicly open to automated abuse
- Write a curl command for every endpoint and run each one — verify every endpoint returns the correct response or the correct error code before moving on

**Submission:**
- Complete FastAPI backend with session management, SQLite request logging, rate limiting, product-specific feedback endpoint, API key auth, and curl verification output for every endpoint

title: Integrate Advanced Retrieval into Your Product
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Advanced Retrieval Implementation
Basic retrieval just fetches the top-k most similar chunks. Advanced retrieval understands that the query may need rewriting, retrieved results may need re-ranking, and sometimes a hypothetical answer is a better search vector than the literal question. Implement three advanced retrieval techniques in your product and measure each one's impact on quality.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API, LangChain, FAISS

**What to do:**
- Implement HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embedding): before searching the index, ask the LLM to generate a short hypothetical answer to the query, embed that hypothetical answer, and use it as the search vector instead of the raw query
- Implement query rewriting for conversational follow-ups: if conversation history is not empty, send the latest query plus the last two turns to the LLM and ask it to rewrite the query as a fully self-contained standalone question before embedding
- Implement a re-ranking step: retrieve the top 10 chunks by cosine similarity, send all 10 to the LLM with the original query, and ask it to select and rank the three most relevant — use only those three as generation context
- Test HyDE against standard retrieval on five queries from your Day 41 example set where the phrasing is indirect or conversational rather than keyword-like
- Test query rewriting on five conversational follow-up queries that use pronouns or vague references to something mentioned in a previous turn
- Score the quality of each technique's output using your Day 29 LLM judge framework before and after adding each technique
- Decide which techniques to keep based on quality improvement divided by latency cost — document your reasoning for each keep-or-drop decision

**Submission:**
- Advanced retrieval pipeline with HyDE, query rewriting, and re-ranking, LLM judge quality scores before and after each technique, and documented keep-or-drop decisions

title: Prompt Engineering for Production Reliability
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Production Prompt Design
Prompts that work 90% of the time fail 10% of the time — and in production, that 10% is what gets screenshotted and shared. Engineer your product's prompts to handle edge cases explicitly, enforce output format consistency, and behave predictably across the full range of realistic user inputs, not just the ones you designed for.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API

**What to do:**
- Run your current system prompt against 20 diverse inputs including five deliberate edge cases: empty retrieved context, two conflicting source documents, an out-of-domain question, a query over 500 characters, and a question with multiple valid interpretations
- Redesign the system prompt with clearly separated sections: Role, Instructions, Constraints, Output Format, and Examples — write each section as an explicit labelled block
- Add a specific handling instruction for each of the five edge cases directly in the prompt — tell the model exactly what to do rather than relying on implicit behaviour
- Write a validate_response_format(response) function that checks whether the LLM response conforms to your required output structure and triggers one automatic retry if validation fails
- Test the redesigned prompt against your Day 29 evaluation dataset and compare the groundedness, correctness, and completeness scores between v1 and v2
- Store both prompt versions in a prompts.py file with a version string and their evaluation scores as inline comments so the history is preserved
- Write a three-sentence note for each edge case instruction explaining what the model did without it and why that behaviour was unacceptable for your product

**Submission:**
- v2 production system prompt with five edge case instructions, validate_response_format() function, v1-vs-v2 eval score comparison table, prompts.py version file with scores as comments, and edge case rationale notes

title: Build Product-Specific Memory and Personalisation
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Memory and Personalisation Systems
The generic conversation memory from Day 24 stores raw message history. Your product needs memory that understands what is worth remembering about each specific user — their preferences, their progress, their past questions — in a way that is tailored to your product's domain and useful across sessions, not just within one conversation.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API, FastAPI, SQLite

**What to do:**
- Define exactly what your product should remember about each user — be concrete and domain-specific: a learning tool might track mastered concepts, a research assistant might track cited sources, a support tool might track resolved issues
- Create a UserProfile table in SQLite with user_id, created_at, and the product-specific memory fields you defined — write create, retrieve, and update functions for this table
- Add a memory read step to your pipeline: before generating any response, query the user's profile and inject the most relevant profile fields into the system prompt as personalisation context
- Add a memory write step: after each interaction, run a short LLM call to extract any new learnable information from the exchange and update the user's profile with the result
- Build a summarise_user_history(user_id) function that triggers when a user exceeds 20 interactions and compresses their full history into a concise user model stored back in the profile
- Run the same query for two users with different stored profiles and print both responses side by side to verify personalisation is genuinely affecting the output
- Write a privacy documentation section: what data you store per user, the legal basis for storing it, and the exact steps a user would take to request deletion of all their data

**Submission:**
- SQLite UserProfile system with memory read and write steps wired into the pipeline, summarise_user_history() function, two-user personalisation comparison output, and privacy documentation

problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Frontend User Experience
AI capability without usable UX produces a product that engineers demo and real users abandon. Today is a focused frontend sprint: build the user interface for your specific AI product — not a generic chat box, but a layout and interaction pattern designed around what your product actually does and how your target user thinks.

**Tools:** Next.js, React, Tailwind CSS

**What to do:**
- Design the primary UI layout specifically for your product's use case — if it is a document assistant show the document alongside the chat, if it is a research tool make sources visually prominent, if it is a learning tool show progress indicators alongside responses
- Implement streaming token rendering using the Fetch API's ReadableStream — verify it works end-to-end in the browser before continuing to other tasks
- Build three explicit loading states: a skeleton loader before the first token arrives, a typing indicator while tokens stream in, and a completion state showing the full response with all metadata
- Build a named error component for each of your API error codes from Day 45 — each must show a human-readable message explaining what happened and a suggested next action, not a raw error code
- Display source citations in a collapsible panel below each response showing the document name, the relevant chunk excerpt, and the similarity score
- Add a feedback mechanism appropriate to your product — thumbs up/down, a rating scale, or a short free-text field — wired to your Day 45 feedback endpoint
- Sit with a non-developer for 15 minutes and watch them use the product without helping — document every moment of confusion and fix the three most critical friction points before the day ends

**Submission:**
- Purpose-built Next.js product frontend with streaming, three loading states, named error components per error code, source citation panel, feedback mechanism, and documented friction log with three fixes applied

title: Evaluate Your AI Product with a Domain-Specific Eval Suite
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Product-Specific Evaluation
Generic NLP benchmarks do not tell you whether your product solves your specific user's problem. Build a 30-question evaluation suite designed around your product's actual use case — covering the easy wins your system should always get right, the medium cases requiring synthesis, and the hard boundary cases that reveal where the system still breaks.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API

**What to do:**
- Build a 30-question evaluation dataset divided into three tiers: 10 easy questions directly answerable from a single chunk in your knowledge base, 10 medium questions requiring synthesis across two or more chunks, and 10 hard questions at the boundary of your product's scope or requiring multi-step reasoning
- Include at least three adversarial questions designed to surface weaknesses: misleading phrasing, a question with no good answer in the knowledge base, and a question likely to cause hallucination
- Run your product against all 30 questions and score each response using your Day 29 LLM judge adapted with evaluation criteria specific to your product's domain
- Calculate scores broken down by tier — easy, medium, and hard separately — and identify which tier your system performs worst on
- Find the five lowest-scoring questions and diagnose each failure: is the problem in retrieval, in the prompt, or in generation — write one sentence per failure with the specific diagnosis
- Fix at least three of the five lowest-scoring failures and re-evaluate those specific questions to confirm each score improved after the fix
- Write a regression_test_runner() that runs the full 30-question suite, writes results to a timestamped file, and prints PASS if all dimensions meet your minimum threshold or FAIL with the specific dimension and score that dropped

**Submission:**
- 30-question domain-specific eval suite with tier breakdown, LLM judge scores per question, five failure diagnoses, three implemented fixes with re-evaluation scores, and regression_test_runner() function

title: Handle Edge Cases and Build Failure Resilience
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Robustness Engineering
Production AI products encounter inputs they were never designed for. Edge cases are not exceptional — they are inevitable once real users start interacting with your product. Systematically identify, categorise, and handle the edge cases specific to your product so every unexpected input results in a helpful response rather than a crash or a silent failure.

**Tools:** Python, FastAPI, OpenAI API

**What to do:**
- Generate an edge case inventory of 20 specific inputs your product might receive outside its intended use: empty queries, single-word inputs, extremely long queries, queries in a different language, and domain-specific boundary cases for your product
- Categorise all 20 edge cases into five groups: out-of-domain, malformed input, adversarial, boundary condition, and extreme length — at least three examples per group
- Run all 20 edge cases through your current system and classify each result as handled gracefully, returned wrong answer, caused an error, or caused a crash
- Implement a specific handler for every case that caused an error or crash — each handler must return a helpful message telling the user what to do rather than exposing a technical stack trace
- Write an input normalisation function that runs before all other processing: strip leading and trailing whitespace, collapse multiple spaces, and handle common Unicode lookalike characters
- Test your out-of-scope refusal behaviour on five queries clearly outside your product's domain — verify the response is politely helpful and suggests what the user should do instead
- Re-run all 20 edge cases after adding handlers and normalisation, then re-run your Day 50 eval suite to confirm none of the 30 standard queries regressed

**Submission:**
- 20-item edge case inventory with category breakdown, before-and-after classification for all 20 cases, implemented handlers, input normalisation function, out-of-scope refusal test outputs, and Day 50 regression check

title: Optimise Your Product for Speed and Cost
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Performance Optimisation for Production
A product that costs ten cents per query and takes five seconds to respond cannot scale to real usage. Measure your current cost and latency baseline precisely, identify the highest-impact optimisation opportunities, implement at least two, and verify that output quality has not dropped below your Day 50 evaluation baseline.

**Tools:** Python, OpenAI API, FastAPI, Redis

**What to do:**
- Profile your complete pipeline end-to-end: log the token count and cost for every OpenAI API call and the time in milliseconds for every processing step across 20 sample queries
- Calculate average cost per query from your token logs and project monthly cost at three usage levels: 1000, 10000, and 100000 queries per day
- Identify your top three cost drivers and top two latency bottlenecks from the profiling data — write one sentence explaining why each is expensive or slow
- Implement semantic response caching using Redis: embed incoming queries, compare against cached query embeddings, and return the cached answer directly if cosine similarity exceeds 0.92
- Implement at least one additional optimisation chosen from: async parallel execution of independent pipeline steps, model downgrade for non-critical sub-steps, chunk size reduction to decrease context tokens, or stricter retrieval score thresholds to reduce prompt size
- Run your Day 50 eval suite after each optimisation and record the quality score — stop optimising if any dimension drops more than 0.3 below the baseline
- Produce a cost-latency-quality comparison table showing average cost per query, average latency, and eval score before and after all optimisations combined

**Submission:**
- Pipeline cost and latency profiling report, semantic caching implementation, one additional optimisation, Day 50 eval scores before and after, and cost-latency-quality comparison table

title: Deploy Your AI Product to Production
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Production Deployment with CI/CD
Day 37 deployed a demo. Day 53 deploys a production-ready product. A production deployment has automated testing before every release, separate staging and production environments, a monitored health endpoint, and a documented rollback procedure. Build a deployment pipeline where shipping a new version is a controlled, repeatable process — not a manual step that requires crossed fingers.

**Tools:** Docker, Railway, Vercel, GitHub Actions

**What to do:**
- Write a GitHub Actions workflow file that triggers on every push to main: runs your full pytest suite, then runs your Day 50 eval regression test, and only proceeds to deployment if both pass with no failures
- Configure a staging environment in Railway with its own environment variables and its own FAISS index — deploy there first and verify behaviour before promoting to production
- Deploy the latest backend version through the GitHub Actions pipeline and confirm every step shows green in the GitHub Actions dashboard before checking the live URL
- Deploy the latest frontend version to Vercel linked to the same GitHub repository and confirm the production frontend connects to the production backend URL
- Document your rollback procedure: the exact Railway and Vercel dashboard steps required to revert to the previous working deployment — test the rollback once and confirm the live product continues working
- Set up uptime monitoring on your production /health endpoint using UptimeRobot's free tier — configure it to check every five minutes and send an email alert on failure
- Simulate a bad deployment: push a commit that deliberately breaks a test, watch the GitHub Actions pipeline block the deploy, revert the commit, and confirm production was never affected throughout

**Submission:**
- GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with pytest and eval gates, staging and production environments on Railway, uptime monitoring configured, rollback procedure documented and tested, and bad-deploy simulation walkthrough

title: Collect and Analyse Real User Feedback
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** User Research and Feedback Analysis
Your product is live and real users can reach it. This is the moment where engineering meets reality. Share the product actively with at least 10 real people, collect structured feedback through the system you built, and convert what you observe into a prioritised improvement list backed by evidence rather than instinct.

**Tools:** Python, FastAPI, SQLite

**What to do:**
- Share your live product URL with at least 10 people outside the ABTalks community — reach out to colleagues, friends in the relevant domain, or topic-specific communities where your target user spends time
- Write a one-paragraph plain-language description of your product and what it does — something a non-technical person would understand and find compelling enough to click
- Monitor your feedback database throughout the day as users interact: watch ratings arrive and note which queries trigger the lowest scores in real time
- After 24 hours, run your feedback analytics script and produce a summary: total queries, positive rating percentage, most common query topics, and most common failure patterns
- Interview two users who gave negative ratings: ask them to show you what they tried to do, what they expected to happen, and what actually happened — write down their exact words
- Write a feedback analysis script that groups all incoming queries by topic using keyword frequency and prints the three topic clusters with the highest failure rates
- Compile a three-item prioritised improvement list: each item must include a specific product change, the supporting evidence from feedback data, and an honest estimate of the engineering effort required

**Submission:**
- Feedback analysis report with 24-hour usage statistics, two user interview notes with verbatim quotes, topic cluster script output, and three-item prioritised improvement list with evidence

title: Technical Review and Final System Hardening
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Technical Review and Code Quality
Before the public launch on Day 58, your system needs a comprehensive technical review. This is the engineering equivalent of a pre-flight checklist — systematically verify every component, test every integration point, and resolve every issue that could embarrass you when real users arrive in volume after the launch post.

**Tools:** Python, FastAPI, pytest, GitHub

**What to do:**
- Run your complete Day 50 eval suite and record the current scores as your official pre-launch baseline — these numbers belong in your launch post
- Do a full codebase read-through and write a list of every issue you find: hardcoded values, missing tests, TODO comments left in, functions without docstrings, and functions over 50 lines that should be split
- Open your Railway production dashboard and verify every environment variable is set to the correct production value — not a test value, not a placeholder, not an empty string
- Run a security sweep: grep the codebase for strings resembling API keys or passwords, confirm rate limiting blocks repeated requests, and verify input validation rejects known injection patterns
- Test the complete end-to-end user journey on the live production environment — not staging — by completing five tasks a real user would do and writing down every issue you encounter
- Make a trivial code change, push to GitHub, and watch the full CI/CD pipeline run in the Actions tab — confirm the deploy completes successfully and the /health endpoint returns 200 after re-deploy
- Write a known limitations document listing what your product cannot do well, at what query volume or complexity it starts degrading, and what you would fix first if you had another two weeks

**Submission:**
- Pre-launch eval baseline scores, written codebase audit list with fixes applied, production environment variable checklist verified, security sweep results, end-to-end production test notes, and known limitations document

title: AI Engineering Interview Preparation
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Interview Readiness
Technical interviews for AI engineering roles test system design depth, implementation fluency, and the ability to communicate trade-offs under pressure. Prepare for the specific question types that come up in AI product interviews by practising against your own system — the work you have done for 57 days is your best preparation material.

**Tools:** Notion, Loom, GitHub

**What to do:**
- Write full written answers to five system design questions as if responding in a real interview: design a customer support AI for a SaaS company; how would you reduce hallucination in a medical information assistant; your RAG system returns wrong answers 20% of the time — walk through your debugging process step by step; how would you scale from 100 to 100000 daily users; what evaluation metrics would you use to measure whether your assistant is genuinely improving over time
- Do a 30-minute mock interview with a peer: they ask three questions, you answer verbally without notes, they give specific feedback on clarity and depth — then swap roles
- Review your mock interview and identify three specific moments where you knew the concept but struggled to explain it clearly — write a cleaner version of each answer
- Prepare a five-minute live code walkthrough of the most technically impressive component you built — practise explaining every design decision as you navigate the code
- Write three STAR-format behavioural answers covering engineering challenges from this programme: one about a technical decision made under uncertainty, one about a failure you diagnosed and fixed, one about an improvement you drove using data
- Record yourself answering 'Walk me through your AI product' in under three minutes — watch it back, note what was unclear or too slow, and record an improved second version
- Build a one-page technical cheat sheet of the 15 most important concepts from this programme written entirely in your own words — use it as a review document before any AI engineering interview

title: Public Product Launch Day
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Public Launch and Distribution
Building in private has no leverage. Public launch means your product is accessible to anyone, your engineering decisions are visible and accountable, and real users outside your network can discover what you built. Execute a structured launch — product live, demo visible, story told authentically, community reached with substance.

**Tools:** GitHub, LinkedIn, Twitter

**What to do:**
- Run a complete pre-launch verification: check all endpoints return healthy responses, re-run the eval suite to confirm scores meet your minimum threshold, and load the frontend on both desktop and mobile to confirm nothing is broken
- Write a LinkedIn launch post that tells the honest engineering story: the problem you noticed, how the system works at an architectural level, one specific engineering challenge you had to work through, your evaluation score, and a direct link to try it
- Post on Twitter with a different angle — keep it short, lead with the live product link, include a screen recording or screenshot of the product in action, and mention the core tech stack
- Share the product in at least two communities relevant to your domain — a relevant subreddit, a Discord server, a Slack community, or a topic-specific forum — writing a context-appropriate description rather than copying the LinkedIn post
- Reply to every comment and direct message in the first 24 hours with a specific, substantive response — engage with actual questions and feedback, not generic thank-yous
- Check your SQLite feedback database and /feedback/summary endpoint every few hours and log the running metrics: total interactions, positive rating percentage, and any unexpected error patterns
- At the end of 24 hours, write a launch retrospective: total interactions received, the most common query topic, the most surprising thing a real user tried to do, and one thing you would do differently in the launch

**Submission:**
- Published LinkedIn launch post with live product link, Twitter post with screenshot, two community posts, 24-hour interaction metrics from the database, and a written launch retrospective

title: Community Showcase and Peer Review
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Community Learning and Knowledge Sharing
The ABTalks community has 59 days of parallel engineering work to learn from. Presenting your product to peers forces you to articulate decisions you made automatically. Reviewing other products develops the architectural pattern recognition that comes from seeing multiple different approaches to the same set of problems.

**Tools:** GitHub, LinkedIn, Loom

**What to do:**
- Prepare and deliver a structured 10-minute demo to the ABTalks community covering: the user problem, the system architecture in one diagram, three specific engineering decisions and why you made them, your evaluation scores, and the one thing you would build differently starting today
- Write substantive technical reviews for three other community members' products — for each one identify one architectural strength, ask one specific technical question about a decision they made, and suggest one concrete improvement with a reason
- Read every peer review your product received and document the most technically interesting piece of feedback — write two to three sentences explaining whether you agree and what you would do about it
- Find two peers who built products in a similar domain and compare your architectural choices directly: what retrieval strategy did they use, how did they handle memory, how did they evaluate quality — document the key differences in a short comparison note
- Compile a list of five specific engineering techniques you observed in other products that you had not considered — for each one write a sentence about whether and how you could apply it to your product
- Write a 300 to 400 word reflection: if you started this challenge today with your current knowledge, what would you do differently in the first week, the second week, and the final phase
- Apply at least one improvement suggested by peer review to your codebase, push the change, and confirm the deployment pipeline runs successfully — keeping the review loop real rather than theoretical

**Submission:**
- 10-minute community demo delivered, three written peer reviews, peer feedback response document, comparative architecture notes with two peers, five technique list, reflection document, and one peer-review improvement applied and deployed

Spent the day reviewing my peers' builds and having mine reviewed. This was worth more than a week of solo work.

Reviewed three products from other ABTalks participants. Gave specific, technical feedback on each: not "looks good" but "your retrieval threshold is set too high — lower it to 0.7 and test on these five query types." Also identified two architectural patterns from their work that I had not used and want to.

Got back equally specific feedback on my own product. Two pieces of feedback identified real issues. One I had noticed and not fixed. One I genuinely had not seen.

This is what a technical community is for.

Day 59 of the ABTalks AI Engineering Challenge. Thank you Anil Bajpai Sir and the ABTalks community for building something that makes peer review feel like the most valuable part of the program. abtalks.in

title: AI Engineer Graduation — Transformation Showcase
problemStatement:
**Focus Area:** Completion, Reflection, and Next Steps
60 days is not the end of an AI engineering journey — it is the foundation. Day 60 is for documenting the transformation completely, sharing everything publicly, and defining what comes next with enough specificity that it actually happens. The engineer who completes Day 60 can articulate not just what they built, but why every major decision was made, what broke, and how they think about AI systems differently now than they did on Day 8.

**Tools:** GitHub, LinkedIn, Loom

**What to do:**
- Write a complete engineering retrospective: what you built across all 60 days, the five most important technical lessons, the three biggest failures and specifically what each one taught you, and what you would do differently starting from Day 1 with your current knowledge
- Record a 10-minute graduation demo using Loom: show the full product working in production, walk through the architecture diagram explaining each component's role, discuss three engineering trade-offs you made and whether you stand by them now, and share your final evaluation scores
- Update every public artefact to reflect 60-day quality: the GitHub repository README with final architecture and scores, the LinkedIn profile skills and projects section, and the portfolio page with live links
- Write your next 30 days plan with enough specificity to hold yourself accountable: which AI engineering skill you will deepen, which product you will improve or extend, which community you will contribute to, and a weekly commitment you can measure
- Write a guide for the next cohort starting Day 1: your three most important pieces of advice, the two biggest mistakes you made that they should avoid, and the one mindset shift that changed how you approached this challenge
- Identify three concrete opportunities in your professional or personal domain where the AI engineering skills you built could create immediate, practical value — write one paragraph for each describing the problem and your proposed approach
- Publish your graduation retrospective as a full LinkedIn article — not a short post — with the 10-minute demo video embedded, your evaluation scores included, and a specific call to action for the next person considering this challenge

**Submission:**
- Engineering retrospective document, 10-minute Loom graduation demo published, all public artefacts updated with final scores, next-30-day plan, cohort starter guide, three opportunity analyses, and graduation LinkedIn article published
